A 54-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to your office for evaluation of low bone density and the pending administration of an aromatase inhibitor for breast cancer. Estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer was recently diagnosed after a routine mammogram revealed the presence of a breast mass. She underwent

bilateral mastectomy 2 months ago, and sentinel lymph node biopsy at the time of surgery was negative. Her oncologist is planning to begin therapy with anastrozole. A recent bone density test revealed a femoral neck T score of -2.2 and a lumbar spine T score of -1.9. She has no history of fractures or height loss. Other than a daily multivitamin, she takes no medications.

On physical examination, weight is 160 lb (72.7 kg) and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 27.5 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 130/68 mm Hg, and heart rate is 75 beats/min. She appears well, and her examination findings are notable only for mastectomy scars.

Results from a complete blood cell count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid function tests are within normal limits. Her 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentration is 40 ng/mL (99.8 nmol/L).

Which of the following should be initiated along with the anastrozole?
options: No additional treatment 24 A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and vision problems, both of which have progressively worsened over the last 6 months. Head CT reveals a large sellar mass that extends suprasellarly and distorts the optic chiasm. The mass extends into the clivus and cavernous sinuses bilaterally. The patient has no relevant medical history and takes no medications. On further questioning, he describes noticing reduced libido and sexual dysfunction for about 1 year.
items.explanation:
3 ANSWER: C) Refer the patient for ethanol ablation under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography. A typical approach to insulinoma would be to consider enucleation and, in normal circumstances, Answer D would be correct. However, there are several unusual features of this patient's presentation. Typically, insulinoma does not cause an elevation in β-cell polypeptides that is as marked as that observed in this vignette and elevations of such magnitude should raise the possibility that hypoglycemia is caused by insulin antibodies. However, this possibility is excluded by undetectable insulin antibodies. There is a strong possibility that this insulinoma is malignant given its size, behavior, and inhomogeneous appearance. Their relatively slow growth often makes chemotherapy ineffective and in the absence of a tissue diagnosis, initiating chemotherapy (Answer A) is incorrect. The proximity to the main pancreatic duct and the location in the head strongly suggests that enucleation (Answer D) is not technically possible. Moreover, a Whipple procedure or any pancreatic procedure in the presence of fat infiltration places the patient at high risk of complications such as pancreatic fistula or anastomotic breakdown.

A staged approach was taken in this case, using intervention to decrease unregulated insulin secretion from the insulinoma and to permit weight loss before definitive surgery. Diazoxide and somatostatin were not viable options in this patient, primarily because of cost and, in the case of somatostatin, the patient was unwilling to sell inject 3 times daily. Given some previous success with ethanol ablation (Answer C), this approach was undertaken with subsequent short-term benefit in that the patient was able to fast for periods of 4 to 6 hours without clinically significant symptoms. He was also able to avoid nocturnal snacking and this led to some weight loss (~20 lb [9.1 kg]). Ethanol injection is performed under the guidance of endoscopic ultrasonography. Typically between 0.5 and 1.0 mL are injected and, especially for large tumors, more than one treatment session is required.

Weight loss (Answer B) may improve the ease with which pancreaticoduodenectomy proceeds and may decrease the risk of operative complications. However, impairing this patient's ability to eat and using caloric intake to treat or prevent hypoglycemia is not the best way forward.

Several case reports suggest that verapamil (Answer E) may help prevent hypoglycemia in patients with insulinoma. Many patients referred to tertiary care centers for the treatment of insulinoma have been prescribed this medication with poor results necessitating surgical treatment.

##### Educational Objective:

Evaluate management options for a large insulinoma in the head of the pancreas.

items.question:
A 49-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus of 18 years' duration, hyperlipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and obesity presents for steadily worsening nausea and abdominal pain over the last 72 hours without any obvious exacerbating factors. The pain is primarily epigastric in location and seems worse with eating, although it has become more constant in the past day. He has started to run a low-grade fever (101°F [38.3°C]) and has had 2 episodes of nonbloody bilious emesis in the past 24 hours.

He currently follows an insulin therapy regimen with insulin detemir, 60 units twice daily, and insulin aspart, 10 units before each meal. He takes simvastatin for elevated LDL cholesterol diagnosed 2 years ago and lansoprazole to manage gastroesophageal reflux disease. He typically consumes 2 to 3 alcoholic beverages per day but has not done so since the onset of abdominal pain. In recent weeks, he has been frequently consuming fast-food meals and, as in the past, has occasionally been nonadherent with his insulin therapy. His blood glucose values over the past 72 hours ranged from 220 to 350 mg/dL (12.21 to 19.43 mmol/L).

On physical examination, he has central obesity, but no evidence of abdominal striae. Weight is 190 lb (86.4 kg), and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 31.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 135/95 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 76 beats/min. He has acanthosis nigricans. The thyroid gland is normal without nodularity. Abdominal examination reveals marked epigastric and right upper quadrant tenderness without rebound but with some guarding. Bowel sounds are diminished. No masses are identified. He has a rash consisting of reddish-yellow lesions on the backs of both legs, on the buttocks, and on his knees. The retinal blood vessels show a creamy appearance on fundoscopic examination.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} = 13.5\% $ (0.135)

Creatinine = 0.8 mg/dL (70.7 μmol/L)

Sodium = 128 mEq/L (128 mmol/L)

Potassium = 4.5 mEq/L (4.5 mmol/L)

White blood cell count = 28,500/μL (25.5 × 10⁹/L) with a left shift TSH = 1.5 mIU/L

Which of the following is the most likely cause of his current symptoms?
items.explanation:
7 ANSWER: B) Pancreatitis

Acute pancreatitis (Answer B) is characterized by inflammation of the pancreas with extension of the inflammatory response to other abdominal sites. The severity of acute pancreatitis is variable—mild-to-moderate abdominal pain, pancreatic necrosis, and even catastrophic abdominal inflammation and death. The cause incidence of acute pancreatitis ranges from 4.9 to 35 per 100,000 persons, and it is a leading cause of hospitalization in the United States. Numerous factors predispose an individual to acute pancreatitis including any disease/obstruction, alcohol, hypercalcemia, and hypertriglyceridemia (as was the case in this vignette). Uncontrolled hyperglycemia in the setting of diabetes promotes the risk of hypertriglyceridemia-induced creatitis. Insulin, along with thyroid hormone, is an important regulator of lipoprotein lipase activity, a key one involved in the processing of triglyceride-rich particles including chylomicrons and VLDL. Either insurance or insulin deficiency reduces lipoprotein lipase activity and results in hypertriglyceridemia. Triglycerin�ations of 1000 mg/dL or higher (≥11.30 mmol/L) increase the risk of acute pancreatitis, although closely lower levels do not confer protection. Children with inherited disorders of lipoprotein metabolism increased risk of hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis. Patients with pancreatitis associated with glyceridemia usually present with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Typical findings on physical include eruptive xanthomas over the extensor surfaces of the extremities, buttocks, and back; lipemia and hepatosplenomegaly from fatty infiltration of the liver.

enteric ischemia (Answer E) results from decreased blood flow through the intestinal vasculature. Chronic ischemia may present with “intestinal angina” and is characterized by recurrent acute episodes of pain during periods of heightened intestinal demand, such as after oral intake. Affected patients can crampy, postprandial epigastric pain, usually within an hour of eating. Subsequently, the pain subsides.

Some affected patients develop a food aversion due to the association with pain and subsequently eight loss. Patients with foregut ischemia from celiac artery insufficiency may have nausea, vomiting.

Cholecystitis (Answer D) is an acute inflammation of the gall bladder. Symptoms usually include steady, worsening right upper quadrant pain with associated guarding, fever, and nausea. Affected patients usually have leukocytosis and a positive Murphy sign, which is associated with increased pain on palpation of the gall bladder, and patients catching their breath during inspiration. Such patients are usually ill appearing and lay still because the pain is exacerbated by movement. The episode of pain lasts for several hours and is often aggravated by consumption of a fatty meal.

Gastroparesis (Answer A) is an autonomic complication in patients with diabetes and it is associated with nausea and early satiety. Affected patients are unlikely to present with abdominal pain.

Inflammatory bowel disease, including ulcerative colitis (Answer C), occurs at a higher incidence in patients with other autoimmune conditions. Like pancreatitis, ulcerative colitis can present with acute abdominal pain and signs of inflammation or infection. Usually there is a history of bloody diarrhea, or at least very frequent stools with mucus, which would not be the case in acute pancreatitis. The classic rash associated with ulcerative colitis is pyoderma gangrenosa, an ulcerating rash that tends to spread slowly.

##### Educational Objective:

Diagnose hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis in a patient with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Hypertriglyceridemia-induced acute pancreatitis

Fortson MR, Freedman SN, Web

items.question:
A 64-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus returns to see you for an appointment to reassess management of his peripheral neuropathy. He has managed his diabetes with the use of an insulin pump for the past 8 years and has had no notable problems related to the use of the device. He monitors his fingerstick blood glucose levels 6 to 8 times daily and keeping a carefully documented log of the results. Review of his log reveals that he has occasional nonsevere hypoglycemia during periods of prolonged exertion. He also has blood glucose readings above 200 mg/dL (11.10 mmol/L) 2 to 3 hours after his evening meal several times each week.

The patient's diabetes is complicated by painful peripheral neuropathy. He describes stinging and burning sensations in both lower legs and feet. The discomfort is interfering with his sleep because it is more noticeable at night and is aggravated by touch from sheets or blankets. Three months ago, he began pregabalin at a dosage of 50 mg orally 3 times daily, which he increased to 100 mg 3 times daily after 1 week. He has tolerated the medication well and reports good adherence to therapy; however, he has had no noticeable improvement in his neuropathic symptoms.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 80 beats/min. Height is 74 in (188 cm), and weight is 208 lb (94.5 kg) (BMI = 26.7 kg/m $ ^{2} $). He has no obvious foot deformities or foot ulcerations, and his ability to sense a monofilament in his feet is well preserved. His Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=7.2\% $ (0.072)

Glomerular filtration rate = 78.6 mL/min per 1.73 m²

TSH = 1.40 mIU/L

You review with the patient appropriate strategies to reduce the risk of exercise-induced hypoglycemia, including use of temporarily lower basal rates or suspension of insulin infusion via the pump.

Which of the following is the best therapeutic intervention for this patient's diabetic neuropathic pain?
options: Adjust pump settings to intensify glycemic control
Add duloxetine to his pregabalin therapy
Add amitriptyline to his pregabalin therapy
Change from pregabalin to duloxetine therapy
Change from pregabalin to gabapentin therapy
items.explanation:
## 34 ANSWER: D) Change from pregabalin to duloxetine therapy

34 A. Approximately one-half of persons with diabetes develop peripheral neuropathy at some point in the course of their disease. The most common clinical manifestations are symptoms such as numbness and pain, typically symmetric and developing in the feet but later advancing proximally. Weakness due to motor involvement may occur but is less common than sensory deficits. Not all individuals with peripheral neuropathy experience neuropathic pain; however, when this does occur, it may adversely affect functional status and quality of life. Approximately 10% to 20% of persons with diabetes have painful peripheral neuropathy, although this may be an underestimate because patients do not consistently report their discomfort. Thus, many patients with clinical significant neuropathic pain are most likely untreated or inadequately treated.

Painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a result of damage to small, unmyelinated fibers rather than the large myelinated fibers responsible for proprioception and touch. Thus, as is the case for this patient, marked neuropathic damage may be present despite preservation of the latter senses. Normal responses to testing with monofilament, tuning fork, or even electrophysiologic testing do not exclude the possibility of painful diabetic neuropathy. Hyperglycemia contributes to the development of neuropathy in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, the pathophysiology that leads to this complication is most likely quite complex. In type 2 diabetes, concomitant conditions including hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance at the level of the neuron are probably contributory. More intensive glycemic control appears to significantly reduce the development of neuropathy in persons with type 1 diabetes, but it has not been consistently documented to be as successful in preventing this complication in type 2 diabetes. Importantly, improvement in hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ does not reduce neuropathic pain and, in fact, very rapid normalization of glycemia may in some instances worsen pain. Although intensification this patient's glycemic control (Answer A) may be otherwise indicated, this intervention would not be expected to improve his neuropathic symptoms.

Recent evidence-based guidelines for the treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy have been outlined by groups including the American Academies of Neurology, the American Association of Neuromuscular and Electrodiagnostic Medicine, and the American Association of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation. This set of guidelines and subsequently published review articles describe several first-line classes of drugs as effective treatment of painful diabetic neuropathy. These include voltage-gated calcium channel  $ \alpha_{2}\delta $ ligand anticonvulsant (pregabalin and gabapentin), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (venlafaxine and duloxetine), and tricyclic antidepressants. Of these agents, pregabalin and duloxetine are approved by the US Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Any of these drug classes is appropriate to use as initial therapy for the management of this condition. If on the maximally effective and/or tolerated dosage of one such medication there is no improvement in neuropathic pain, drug therapy should be changed in a first-line agent of a different drug class. This patient has not experienced any noticeable relief of pain with the maximal appropriate dosage of pregabalin; thus, the addition of duloxetine (Answer B) or amitriptyline (Answer C)

C) would not be the best next steps in management. However, if the patient had experienced partial relief of symptoms (described by some authors as a 30% or greater reduction in pain), then the addition of either of these agents to pregabalin therapy would be indicated. items.question:
A 38-year-old man with a 7-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus presents for help attaining better glycemic control. His diabetes is currently being managed with insulin glargine, 19 units at bedtime, and insulin glulisine, 7 units just before he starts a meal. He tries to keep the carbohydrate content of foods consistent at meal times, and he does not snack between meals or before bed. Supplemental insulin glulisine is also used to lower high glucose levels: 1 unit to lower his glucose 40 mg/dL (2.22 mmol/L) to a target of 120 mg/dL (6.66 mmol/L). His total daily insulin dose averages about 40 units. He has been adhering to recommended home glucose monitoring 4 times daily—before meals and at bedtime. Mild symptoms of hypoglycemia occur 3 times per week on average, and they can occur at any time of day and occasionally overnight. His downloaded glucose meter for the past 3 months reveals a mean glucose concentration of 131 mg/dL (7.27 mmol/L) with a range of 47 to 410 mg/dL (2.61 to 22.76 mmol/L) and a standard deviation of 47 mg/dL (2.61 mmol/L). Glucose levels are mildly elevated and mildly variable throughout the day, with no clear trends in high or low glucose readings, and they are mostly stable overnight.

He is up to date on screening examinations and laboratory studies, and he has no evidence of diabetes complications. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level is 7.5% (0.075) today. A laboratory-measured fasting glucose concentration is 113 mg/dL (6.27 mmol/L) (simultaneous meter glucose reading is 115 mg/dL [6.38 mmol/L]). He is frustrated because he has been following insulin-dosing instructions; he would like to lower his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ to help prevent long-term diabetes complications.

Which of the following strategies would be expected to safely lower his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level?
options: Increase the insulin glargine dose by 1 to 2 units daily
Change his bolus insulin glulisine from set doses to an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of 1:8
Change his correction factor to 1 unit to lower his glucose 30 mg/dL (1.66 mmol/L)
Move his insulin glulisine dose to 15 minutes before meals
Instruct him to eat more high-glycemic index foods
items.explanation:
## 42 ANSWER: D) Move his insulin glulisine dose to 15 minutes before meals

42 $ ^{A} $ This patient's mean glucose concentration of 131 mg/dL (7.27 mmol/L) using measurements tested before meals and at bedtime is lower than one would expect on the basis of his hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level. A hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ reading of 7.5% (0.075) equates to a mean blood glucose concentration of about 165 mg/dL (9.16 mmol/L). This supports the idea that hyperglycemia is occurring at times when glucose levels are not tested, namely in the postprandial state. Continuous glucose monitoring has demonstrated that unrecognized postprandial hyperglycemia is common in patients with type 1 diabetes and that it contributes adversely to overall glycemic control as measured by hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $.

Postprandial hyperglycemia can be confirmed by having patients test fingerstick glucose levels after meals. Peak glucose levels occur 1 to 2 hours after meals, depending on meal composition. Foods with a higher glycemic index (low in fiber and/or higher in simple sugars) lead to a more rapid and higher peak glucose level than do foods with a lower glycemic index (thus, Answer E is incorrect). The maximum insulin level after injection of one of the rapid-acting insulin analogues occurs about 90 minutes after injection. Reduced postprandial glucose excursions are seen if the insulin injection is moved from immediately before the meal to 15 minutes before the meal (thus, Answer D is correct).

The insulin sensitivity factor can be calculated using the following equation: 1800/total daily insulin dose. In this case, with the patient using about 40 units of insulin daily, the calculated sensitivity factor would be 1:45, or 1 unit to drop the glucose concentration 45 mg/dL (2.50 mmol/L). In this vignette, increasing the patient's sensitivity factor to 1:30 (Answer C) could lead to increased hypoglycemia.

Two-hour postmeal glucose levels are a better predictor of cardiovascular events and mortality than fasting glucose levels, and they are a better predictor of retinopathy in type 2 diabetes mellitus than hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $. Postprandial hyperglycemia increases oxidative stress and is associated with endothelial dysfunction in patients with type 2 diabetes. Compared with regular insulin, rapid-acting insulin analogues lead to better postprandial glycemic control in patients with type 1 diabetes and has been shown to improve cardiovascular risk markers. However, randomized prospective trials targeting postprandial hyperglycemia have not shown reductions in cardiovascular outcomes in patients with type 2 diabetes and have not been performed in patients with type 1 diabetes.

Interventions that lower postprandial glucose responses to a meal include use of rapid-acting insulin analogues rather than human regular insulin, the consumption of lower-glycemic index carbohydrates, warming the injection site before an insulin bolus, the use of continuous glucose monitors, and bolusing insulin 15 minutes before meals rather than immediately before. The addition of protein to a meal increases the postmeal glucose response and the late postmeal insulin requirement. Improvements in postprandial glucose levels can be documented by having a patient check postmeal glucose levels before and after making the change in timing of the premeal rapid-acting insulin dose.

Prandial insulin coverage can effectively control postprandial glucose levels either with set insulin doses within fixed carbohydrate amounts at mealtimes or by having patients learn to count the grams of carbohydrates in a meal and adjust the prandial insulin dose based on a calculated insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio. A formula commonly used to calculate an individual patient's insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio is 500/total daily insulin dose. For this patient, 500/40 units daily results in an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of about 1:12 (thus, Answer B is incorrect). Switching him to an insulin-to-carbohydrate ra

The insulin sensitivity factor can be calculated using the following equation: 1800/total daily insulin dose. In this case, with the patient using about 40 units of insulin daily, the calculated sensitivity factor would be 1:45, or 1 unit to drop the glucose concentration 45 mg/dL (2.50 mmol/L). In this vignette, increasing the patient's sensitivity factor to 1:30 (Answer C) could lead to increased hypoglycemia.

Two-hour postmeal glucose levels are a better predictor of cardiovascular events and mortality than fasting glucose levels, and they are a better predictor of retinopathy in type 2 diabetes mellitus than hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $. Postprandial hyperglycemia increases oxidative stress and is associated with endothelial dysfunction in patients with type 2 diabetes. Compared with regular insulin, rapid-acting insulin analogues lead to better postprandial glycemic control in patients with type 1 diabetes and has been shown to improve cardiovascular risk markers. A 59-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 11 years ago), hypercholesterolemia, and hypertension presents for evaluation of acute-onset pain followed by weakness in her left leg and hip. The pain has been present for 3 weeks and the weakness started 5 days ago. She has not identified exacerbating or relieving factors for her symptoms and did not experience any preceding trauma. The discomfort is aching in quality and only present on the left side. Because of the weakness, she is having difficulty lifting her left leg to get into her car or into bed. Her bowel and bladder function has been normal.

She takes metformin and glyburide for type 2 diabetes mellitus, hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension, and atorvastatin for hypercholesterolemia.

On physical examination, she appears uncomfortable. Weight is 130 lb (59.1 kg), and height is 63 in (160 cm) (BMI = 23 kg/m²). Her weight has decreased by 6 lb (2.7 kg) in the last 3 months. Blood pressure is 100/75 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 86 beats/min. Her thyroid gland is normal without dominant nodularity. She has mild loss of distal sensation on Semmes-Weinstein monofilament testing. Only the left quadriceps reflex is present; other reflexes are absent. Vibration and position sense are normal. You are able to elicit left hip and thigh pain with

movement. There is no pain on palpation of the muscles. There is no tremor of the outstretched hands. Distal pulses are intact and easily palpable.

## 51 ANSWER: A) Diabetic amyotrophy

Diabetic amyotrophy (Answer A) is also known as Bruns-Garland syndrome. Because of the considerable diabetic neuropathy, diabetic polyradiculopathy, diabetic motor neuropathy, diabetic radiculoplexopathy, diabetic lumbosacral plexopathy, and diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy. However, diabetic amyotrophy

affects both the lumbosacral nerve roots and peripheral nerves; thus, it is not a pure lumbosacral plexopathy as is the case with idiopathic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy. Although lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy is high on the differential diagnosis list, it is not associated with diabetes. This, along with the lack of involvement of peripheral nerves, helps distinguish it from diabetic neuropathy.

Diabetic amyotrophy is seen at any stage of diabetes neuropathy. in middle-aged patients with reasonable diabetes control or in those with a recent diabetes diagnosis. Sometimes diabetic amyotrophy can be the presenting feature of diabetes. It is more prevalent in patients with type 2 diabetes or type 1 diabetes. Concomitant weight loss is common. Affected persons usually have unilateral proximal hypoflexia due to the loss of the body'sி nteroverset with the lack of involvement of the type of diabetes in the body.

dyspnea is often found as a subset of patients have no associated pain; weakens; weight loss; and autonomic dysfunction such as orthostatic dysfunction, tachycardia, altered sweating, etc. Upper limb or thoracic involvement may occur. Affected patients may present with asymmetric weakness involving the proximal extremity. However, over time (months), the signs and symptoms progress to the contralateral extremity and become symmetric. Neurophysiologic testing typically shows a predominant axonal neuropathy, and nerve biopsy may document microvasculitis. Neuroimaging can be used to exclude other differential diagnoses. Nerve conduction studies reveal marked reduction in amplitudes of the compound muscle and sensory nerve action potentials, while conduction velocities are not as significant. Treatment includes narcotic analgesic agents, antidepressant medications, and occupational and physical therapy. Immune-modulating therapy or steroid therapy is not helpful. Complete recovery is uncommon, but clinical improvement can be accomplished with antidepressants, physical therapy, patient education, and reassurance.

Vitamin B $ _{12} $ deficiency (Answer B) usually presents with symmetric neuropathy that affects the legs more than the arms. The initial presenting symptom is paresthesias and ataxia associated with loss of vibration and position sense, which may progress to severe weakness, spasticity, clonus, paraplegia, and even fecal and urinary incontinence.

Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (Answer C) generally presents with symmetric weakness with various sensory symptoms that exhibit either a relapsing, stepwise progressive, or steadily progressive course. In addition to limb weakness, affected patients may also present with fatigue, numbness, tingling, or tight sensation in their extremities. Pain is rarely a component of chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Diminished or absent reflexes on neurologic testing is the rule. Cranial nerve involvement occurs in some cases and loss of micturition control due to involvement of pelvic neural circuitry is relatively common. Meralgia paresthetica (Answer D) is characterized by burning pain, tingling, numbness, and decreased sensation over the lateral thigh, corresponding to the distribution of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. This nerve does not contain motor fibers, so affected patients do not have motor deficits such as those observed in the vignette.

items.question:
A 26-year-old man presents to your office accompanied by his wife after he experienced a severe hypoglycemic reaction. Type 1 diabetes mellitus was diagnosed 15 years ago, and he has no known diabetes diagnosis. He has enjoyed the convenience of twice-daily insulin dosing with NPH insulin and insulin lispro

before breakfast and dinner. His current insulin regimen consists of NPH insulin, 20 units, and insulin lispro, 6 units, before breakfast, and NPH insulin, 8 units, and insulin lispro, 8 units, before dinner.

He has been measuring his glucose levels at home 0 to 2 times daily when he feels glucose levels are running high or low. Low glucose levels leading to slowed mentation and fatigue occur several times a week, and they range from 35 to 50 mg/dL (1.94 to 2.78 mmol/L) when tested with symptoms.

He refereed 2 football games on a Saturday afternoon and evening. The next morning, shortly after eating a large breakfast and taking his toddler on a long stroller walk, his wife found him seizing in the child's room. His fingerstick glucose concentration was 26 mg/dL (1.44 mmol/L), and his wife immediately administered glucagon. After a long period of confusion, he refused transfer to an emergency department by an ambulance crew. He recalls only feeling tired before losing consciousness.

On physical examination, findings from his cardiac and peripheral nerve examinations are normal. There are no significant orthostatic changes in blood pressure or pulse rate. His hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level in your office is 6.7% (0.067), and his 11:30-AM fingerstick glucose measurement is 68 mg/dL (3.77 mmol/L).

### In addition to increasing the frequency of home glucose monitoring and placing him on a more physiologic insulin regimen, which of the following is the most important recommendation to make now?
options: Increase his intake of high glycemic index foods
Have him pay greater attention to symptoms of hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia to guide premeal insulin-likeடோ dose adjustments
Change from set insulin lispro doses to an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio of 1 unit to 6 g of carbohydrate
Raise glycemic targets in the short-term
items.explanation:
## 76 ANSWER: E) Raise glycemic targets in the short-term

76 Hypoglycemic unawareness is a common problem in patients with tightly controlled type 1 diabetes. Loss of adrenergic symptoms of hypoglycemia is more frequent with increasing duration of diabetes, and it is a major risk factor for severe hypoglycemia requiring assistance. Recurrent hypoglycemia reduces the normal counterregulatory hormone response of glucagon and epinephrine secretion to low glucose levels and decreases hypoglycemia awareness. Antecedent hypoglycemia was identified in the Diabetes Control and Complications Trial as the most common predictor of a future severe hypoglycemic event.

Although this patient's hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ level has run consistently in a good range, his hypoglycemic unawareness indicates that good mean glucose levels are most likely occurring at the expense of recurrent hypoglycemia. Prevention of future severe hypoglycemia, a potentially life-threatening complication of insulin

therapy, should be improved by hypoglycemia for periods as short as 3 days has been shown to improve hypoglycemia awareness for up to 3 months and therefore reduce the risk of future hypoglycemia. Relaxing glycemic control in the short-term by raising glucose targets to prevent hypoglycemia (Answer E) will help reduce his risk of a recurrent severe hypoglycemic event.

increased frequency of blood glucose monitoring is critical to alert the patient to times of the day when glucose levels are running low and thus guide insulin dose reductions. This patient would be best advised to perform fingerstick glucose monitoring at minimum before meals and at bedtime, before driving, and when he has any symptoms of hypoglycemia (with or without use of a continuous glucose monitor). Studies have documented that patients are often inaccurate when estimating their glucose concentration unless levels are very high or low. Therefore, dosing insulin lispro on the basis of the perceived blood glucose level (Answer C) would not be expected to reduce his frequency of hypoglycemia.

Continuous glucose monitors should be considered for patients with persistent hypoglycemia unawareness because these devices can alert patients to unrecognized hypoglycemia. Continuous glucose monitors have been shown to lower hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ levels in adults and children with type 1 diabetes without increasing the rate of mild or severe hypoglycemia, and have been shown to reduce the frequency of severe hypoglycemia. A continuous glucose monitoring device has been introduced that communicates with an insulin pump and automatically suspends the basal insulin infusion when the glucose level is low and unrecognized. Use of this device reduces the amount of time spent in a hypoglycemic state overnight.

This patient's insulin regimen is nonphysiologic, and he would benefit from a change. Physiologic insulin replacement using a long-acting basal insulin and premeal rapid-acting insulin has been shown to lower the risk of hypoglycemia when compared with NPH-based insulin regimens. Insulin pump therapy, the most physiologic of insulin replacement regimens, would similarly be expected to reduce hypoglycemia rates in this patient. The intake of high glycemic index foods will acutely raise glucose levels, but such a dietary intervention (Answer B) is not an effective way prevent hypoglycemia in a patient on a nonphysiologic insulin regimen, and would lead to excessive postprandial hyperglycemia.

If this patient is found to have marked glucose variability because of variable carbohydrate intake at mealtimes, having him adjust his bolus insulin for anticipated carbohydrate intake using an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio should reduce glucose variability and rates of hypoglycemia. An insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio can be estimated from his total daily dose of insulin. A formula commonly used to calculate an individual patient's insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio is 500/total daily dose. His current total dai

In the preconception counseling of this patient, which of the following should you recommend?

items.explanation:
78 ANSWER: D) Initiate treatment with methyldopa for management of her blood pressure

79 Women with pregestational type 1 diabetes may encounter several risks in pregnancy. Preconception counseling is very important to educate women about these risks and preempt exacerbation of existing complications or the development of new ones.

Uncontrolled hypertension can be a serious complication of pregnancy in women with diabetes and can precipitate retinal, renal, and cardiac dysfunction. ACE inhibitors (Answer C) are teratogenic, so they are intraindicated for this patient who is contemplating pregnancy despite the presence of microalbuminuria. Editions other than ACE inhibitors are the mainstay of hypertension treatment during pregnancy. Specifically, thyldopa (Answer D) has been used for many years, and it is safe for both the mother and developing fetus. Her antihypertensive agents that can safely be used during pregnancy include calcium channel blockers and drenergic blockers such as hydralazine. β-Adrenergic blockers can be used with caution.

Physiologic ocular changes that can occur in pregnancy include alterations of corneal sensitivity and thickness, reased tolerance to contact lenses, decreased intraocular pressure, hermalopia, and refractive errors. Pregnancy exacerbate preexisting retinopathy. Some women with diabetes develop de novo retinopathy during pregnancy, the development of any new ocular symptoms should be assessed promptly. Thus, dilated eye examinations

should ideally be performed before and during the first trimester of pregnancy, even in women such as this one who do not have preexisting retinopathy (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Subsequent retinal examinations should be based on the results of the preliminary examination. Retinal examinations should not be delayed until after completion of the pregnancy, as this strategy would miss de novo retinopathy if it occurs. In the Diabetes in Early Pregnancy Study that examined 140 women without proliferative retinopathy at conception, progression of retinopathy occurred in 20% of those with microaneurysms or mild nonproliferative retinopathy and in 55% of those with moderate-to-severe nonproliferative retinopathy. Ten percent of women

Hyperglycemia during the first trimester of pregnancy is associated with a higher rate of fetal loss. For this and other reasons, avoiding elevated glucose levels is a key preconception goal. However, low hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ values (such as <6.0% [<0.060]) could be associated with significant hypoglycemic events and is generally not ideal for most women with type 1 diabetes (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

Her TSH level is appropriate for pregnancy, and increasing her levothyroxine dosage (Answer E) is not warranted now. However, there will be an anticipated increase in her thyroid requirement once she is pregnant, and this should be managed appropriately.

#### Educational Objective:

Educational provide preconception counseling to women with type 1 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Prepregnancy evaluation and management of women with type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus

A 68-year-old man with a 7-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus is referred to you for progressively uncontrolled hyperglycemia. Diabetes was initially diagnosed when he presented to the hospital with chest pain and he subsequently underwent single coronary stent placement for obstructive coronary artery disease. He is currently taking metformin, 1000 mg twice daily, and glimepiride, 4 mg once daily in the morning. In the past 4 years, his fasting glucose levels have mostly ranged from 130 to 190 mg/dL (7.22-10.54 mmol/L). However, in the past few months, his fasting glucose levels have ranged from 240 to 260 mg/dL (13.32-14.43 mmol/L). He works on his farm for 5 to 6 hours daily. He has rare symptoms of hypoglycemia, most often after skipping lunch, when he feels weak and tired. He has had no severe hypoglycemia.

On physical examination, his blood pressure is 135/64 mm Hg and pulse rate is 57 beats/min. Weight is 152.5 lb (69.3 kg), and height is 66 in (167.7 cm) (BMI 24.6 kg/m²). He has no retinopathy. items.explanation:
## 91 ANSWER: E) Referral to a dietician

91 A Just as the oral glucose tolerance test has no role in the evaluation of hypoglycemia, having a patient who has undergone Roux-en-Y gastric bypass consume a meal with calories in liquid form will most likely precipitate a constellation of symptoms often called “dumping” and low glucose values may be observed. This is a well-known effect of such surgery, and patients should be urged to adhere to dietary instructions after bariatric procedures. Up to 10% of healthy patients exhibit post-oral glucose tolerance test glucose concentrations less than 50 mg/dL without symptoms of neuroglycemia, and the same considerations apply after a patient has had Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. In the postprandial setting, one should be wary of applying fasting criteria for endogenous hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia given that insufficient time has elapsed to clear all secreted  $ \beta $-cell polypeptides from the peripheral circulation. Indeed, this is borne out by the normal results after a mixed meal that meets the criteria for reasonable nutrition after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, a surgical procedure that results in unregulated emptying of liquid from the stomach. In such circumstances, ingestion of a caloric liquid often produces vasomotor symptoms.

For a patient such as the one in this vignette, enlisting the services of a dietician (Answer E) is the best management step. The dietician should be experienced in the care of patients who have undergone bariatric surgery that can lead to dumping. Typically these patients do not have neuroglycopenia. It is important to note that this patient had postprandial blood glucose values between 60 and 70 mg/dL (3.33 and 3.88 mmol/L), but these values may be normal in this setting and there was no accompanying neuroglycopenia. Limited weight loss after Rouxen-Y gastric bypass is often an indicator of poor dietary counseling and/or poor dietary adherence.

Calcium arterial stimulation testing (Answer A) should not be considered. Abnormalities because normative values have not been established for patients after upper gastrointestinal surgery. Calcium arterial stimulation testing should not be undertaken until after a firm diagnosis of a hypoglycemic disorder has been made and surgical therapy is being considered. The poor long-term response to distal pancreatectomy in such situations has cooled enthusiasm for this procedure in such patients. Likewise, endoscopic ultrasonography (Answer B) would only be indicated if evidence of hypoglycemia after a standard mixed meal is present and the Whipple triad is fulfilled. This requires documentation of symptoms that are compatible with neuroglycopenia, which occur in the presence of hypoglycemia and are reversed with amelioration of hypoglycemia.

A 72-hour fast (Answer C) is unlikely to be helpful given that these symptoms usually occur after meals. Diagnostic imaging such as CT (Answer D) and ultrasonography should be performed only after hyperinsulinemia hypoglycemia is confirmed, and they have no role until the diagnosis is established.

Educate hypoglycemia that occurs after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass with a standardized solid mixed-meal challenge.

items.question:
You are asked to evaluate a 72-year-old man with longstanding type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and coronary artery disease who has been admitted to the emergency department for hypoglycemia. The patient was hospitalized 2 months earlier with volume overload and electrolyte abnormalities attributable to end-stage renal disease. He received emergent hemodialysis at that time, and for the past 6 weeks he has been performing peritoneal dialysis at home with an icodextrin-containing dialysate.

Over the past month, the patient has contacted his physician multiple times because of consistent elevations in his self-monitored blood glucose readings. His prescribed insulin doses have been repeatedly increased over this period, with the most recent adjustment occurring several days ago. The patient is now taking insulin detemir at a dosage of 20 units once daily along with 8 units of insulin glulisine at each meal with additional amounts taken if the blood glucose concentration is greater than 150 mg/dL (>8.33 mmol/L). After the most recent increase in his insulin doses, his self-monitored blood glucose values have decreased from an average of 225 mg/dL (12.49 mmol/L) to approximately 175 mg/dL (9.71 mmol/L). This morning, the patient administered 20 units of insulin detemir and a total of 10 units of insulin glulisine before breakfast. Approximately 30 minutes later, emergency medical services were called because he was found to be unresponsive.

The patient is currently afebrile and is not tachycardic or hypoxemic. His blood pressure has improved from 82/44 mm Hg to 126/77 mm Hg with the administration of intravenous fluids. His peritoneal dialysis catheter is intact without surrounding erythema or drainage, and his abdomen is soft and nontender. When he arrived at the emergency department, his serum glucose concentration was 29 mg/dL (1.61 mmol/L). The patient's mental status is now dramatically improved after administration of a 50% dextrose solution.

Which of the following best explains this patient's severe hypoglycemia?
options: Inaccurate glucose meter data due to dialysate type

On physical examination, his blood pressure is 162/91 mm Hg and heart rate is 77 beats/min. Height is 72 in (182.9 cm), and weight is 213 lb (96.8 kg) (BMI = 28.9 kg/m²). He has been treated for the past 4 years with metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and twice-daily insulin glargine. He takes no other medications. Although he previously worked a physically active job, he has not recently exercised. He tries to eat a low-fat diet, moderates his intake of carbohydrates (and tries to consume those that are high in fiber), and consumes an average of 4 fruit and vegetables. Fasting glucose concentrations range mostly between 140 and 180 mg/dL (7.77 and 9.99 mmol/L) and glucose concentrations after breakfast range between 200 and 260 mg/dL (11.10 and 14.43 mmol/L). He takes lisinopril, 10 mg daily, for hypertension. He does not smoke cigarettes. He has no chest or calf claudication pain. Findings on review of systems are otherwise normal.

On physical examination, height is 69 in (175.3 cm) and weight is 175 lb (79.5 kg) (BMI = 25.8 kg/m²). His blood pressure is 148/81 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 84 beats/min. There is no retinopathy on dilated eye examination no carotid or femoral bruits, normal findings on peripheral nerve examination, and normal pedal pulses.

Laboratory test results (sample drawn while fasting):

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=8.7\% $ (0.087)

Glucose = 154 mg/dL (8.55 mmol/L)

Total cholesterol = 190 mg/dL (4.92 mmol/L)

Creatinine = 0.9 mg/dL (79.6 μmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 51 mg/dL (1.32 mmol/L)

Urinary albumin = 233 mg/g creatinine

LDL cholesterol = 80 mg/dL (2.07 mmol/L)

Hepatic function, normal

Triglycerides = 295 mg/dL (3.33 mmol/L)

Which of the following is recommended to reduce cardiovascular risk in this patient?
options: Combination therapy with a statin and a fibrate

His morning total testosterone concentration was 240 ng/dL (8.33 nmol/L). His pretreatment hematocrit was 42% (0.42) and his prostate-specific antigen concentration was 1.2 ng/ml (1.2 μg/L). His prostate was symmetrically enlarged, nontender, without any focal nodule or induration. The patient has been on testosterone therapy for 5 months and feels well. He reports improved sexual function and energy. He is tolerating the testosterone solution without any skin irritation. He has no lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient saw his primary care physician 2 weeks ago for his annual checkup. His hematocrit level was 45.7% (0.457) and his prostate-specific antigen concentration was 2.8 ng/mL (2.8 μg/L).

## 102 ANSWER: C) A statin Hypertriglyceridemia is

102 Hypertriglyceridemia is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, although causality remains unclear. Triglyceride levels above 1000 mg/dL (>11.3 mmol/L) increase the risk of pancreatitis and should be aggressively treated with dietary and lifestyle interventions and usually concomitant pharmacologic therapy with fibrates, niacin, or fish oil.

Patients with diabetes mellitus who have hypertriglyceridemia should be screened for potentially treatable secondary causes such as hypothyroidism; nephrotic syndrome; obesity; alcohol use; and medication use including estrogens, glucocorticoids, cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide, noncardioselective  $ \beta $-adrenergic blockers, tamoxifen, phenothiazines, isotretinoin, and antiretroviral agents.

In patients with modest hypertriglyceridemia, the treatment focus should be nonpharmacologic and include weight loss in obese patients; regular aerobic exercise; and restriction of carbohydrates, especially food high in sugars. Among patients with diabetes, improved glycemic control effectively lowers triglyceride levels. In general, antihyperglycemic agents lower triglyceride levels. Insulin promotes the synthesis of lipoprotein lipase, which hydrolyzes triglycerides to free fatty acids. Intravenous insulin can effectively lower markedly elevated triglycerides. However, lowering triglycerides with prandial insulin has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular risk (thus, Answer E is incorrect).

Several cholesterol-lowering agents effectively lower triglycerides, including brates (18%-45%), niacin (17%-26%), and statins (5%-30%). High-dosage atorvastatin and rosuvastatin have the greatest triglyceride-lowering effect in the statin class. Niacin also raises HDL-cholesterol levels and has a modest LDL-lowering effect, but it has never been shown in monotherapy trials to reduce cardiovascular events rates or mortality among patients with diabetes (thus, Answer D is incorrect). Pharmacologic intervention of isolated hypertriglyceridemia with agents such as fibrates has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular event rates (thus, Answer B is incorrect). The Veterans Affairs High-Density Lipoprotein Intervention Trial (VA-HIT) showed a significant reduction in cardiovascular events among patients treated with gemfibrozil whose primary lipid abnormality was a low HDL-cholesterol level. Treated patients had a 6% increase in HDL cholesterol and a 31% lowering of triglycerides. In the Fenofibrate Intervention and Event Lowering in Diabetes (FIELD) trial, patients older than 50 years who had diabetes mellitus and were not taking lipid-lowering agents were treated with fenofibrate or placebo. After 5 years, treated patients had nonsignificantly lower rates of coronary events, and a nonsignificantly higher rate of coronary death and all-cause mortality. Combination therapy with a statin and fibrate has not been shown to improve cardiovascular risk reduction when compared with treatment with a statin alone (thus, Answer A is incorrect).

In addition to hypertriglyceridemia, this patient's cardiovascular risk factors include hypertension and albuminuria. The American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology cardiovascular risk calculators scores this patient's 10-year cardiovascular risk at 17.6%, well above the 7.5% risk cutoff recommended for initiation of statin therapy, even in individuals whose baseline LDL-cholesterol concentration is well below 100 mg/dL (2.59 mmol/L). Given the high cardiovascular risk in patients with diabetes, the 2013 American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology guidelines recommend that a moderate- or high-intensity statin be started in patients with diabetes who are between 40 and 75 years old and have an LDL-cholesterol concentration of 70 to 189 mg/dL (1.81-4.90 mmol/L) without clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (thus, Answer C is correct).

items.question:
A 22-year-old woman who has been seeing you for the management of hypothyroidism returns to clinic for a scheduled appointment. The patient's younger sister recently received a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes mellitus. Your patient is aware that she may be at increased risk of developing type 1 diabetes herself and seeks your advice regarding screening for this condition. None of her other relatives are known to have type 1 diabetes; however, type 2 diabetes was diagnosed in her maternal grandfather at age 70 years.

The patient has no known medical problems other than a 2-year history of hypothyroidism, which is adequately managed with levothyroxine. She has no history of hospitalizations or surgical interventions and has received all recommended vaccinations. She has had no recent illnesses, and her review of systems is unremarkable. In particular, she notes no changes in her energy level, weight, or menses. She has had no vision changes, polydipsia, polyuria, or nocturia.

On physical examination, the patient is alert and appears well. Blood pressure is 104/72 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. Height is 64 in (162.6 cm), and weight is 115 lb (52.3 kg) (BMI = 19.7 kg/m²). She is normally developed and well nourished. Other than the finding of a slightly enlarged thyroid gland, her physical examination findings are unremarkable.

You explain that screening for type 1 diabetes may not identify all patients who will eventually develop the condition. The patient wishes to proceed with testing nonetheless.

Which of the following is the best next step?
options: Referral to a clinical research study that performs type 1 diabetes screening
Hemoglobin A $ _{1c} $ measurement at today's visit
Measurement of fasting glucose, insulin, and C-peptide levels
Screening for islet-cell cytoplasmic autoantibodies and glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies

Screening for insulinoma-2-associated autoantibodies and zinc transporter 8 autoantibodies From: Endo doctor [endodocctor@healthcare.com] Sent: Wednesday, November 06, 2014 4:05 PM To: compoundF@zonafasciculata.com Subject: interesting patient question Hi, Dr. Compound F, I would like your help with an interesting patient I saw recently at the request of her family physician. This is an unusual case, and I am feeling stuck on the workup. The patient is a 71-year-old woman with hypertension diagnosed at age 37 years. Earlier this year, bilateral renal artery stenosis was diagnosed and she underwent bilateral stenting. I did not meet her until after that procedure. I don't have access to hormonal testing done before the procedure, and she hasn't had imaging studies showing the adrenal glands. Before the stenting, she had a normal serum creatinine level and her chemistry panel was normal except for some intermittent low sodium measurements (as low 126 mEq/L [126 mmol/L] on one occasion, but usually between 130 and 136 mEq/L [130-136 mmol/L]). After the stenting, her uncontrolled hypertension persisted. Her potassium level is just mildly low: 3.3 mEq/L (3.3 mmol/L). Renal ultrasonography showed good patent flow. Her family doctor tested her for secondary causes of hypertension before sending her to me: Urinary catecholamines and metanephrines, normal Plasma renin activity = 22 ng/mL per h Aldosterone = 4.6 ng/dL (127.6 pmol/L) Urinary cortisol = <2.2 $ \mu $g/24 h (6.07 nmol/d) She was sent to me to exclude an adrenal disorder. She had no history or features consistent with low cortisol, blood pressure was 190/70 mm Hg, and heart rate was 120 beats/min (she was nervous; her heart rate did slow down after a while). She appeared very cushingoid with facial rounding, skin thinning with many bruises on her arms, and increased dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat. Her medications included amlodipine, valsartan, atenolol, and hydrochlorothiazide. She recalled taking hydrocodone/acetaminophen 3 months ago for back pain, but had not taken any narcotics for 2 months. I checked a morning serum cortisol level, expecting a normal result. Serum cortisol (9 AM) = 1.6 µg/dL (44.1 nmol/L) ACTH = <5 pg/mL (<1.1 pmol/L) After cosyntropin serum cortisol = 8.1 $ \mu $g/dL (223.5 nmol/L) I also measured a morning salivary cortisol level, which was only $ 0.08\ \mu g/dL $ ( $ 2.1\ nmol/L $)—this must be low sin it is in the normal range for midnight. No pituitary abnormalities were noted on brain MRI done last year. Free $ T_{4} $ and TSH levels were normal. I don't know what to do next and I would appreciate any help you can provide. Thanks. Endo Doc
items.explanation:
107 ANSWER: A) Referral to a clinical research study that performs type 1 diabetes screening. This young woman has a sibling who has recently received a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes; thus, she is understandably concerned regarding her own likelihood of developing diabetes. In general, type 1 diabetes is diagnosed when patients present with acute symptoms of hyperglycemia and possibly diabetic ketoacidosis. There is much interest in predicting which individuals will develop type 1 diabetes with the aim of more closely monitoring at-risk patients and perhaps intervening to prevent or delay disease. However, clear guidelines for the screening of at-risk but asymptomatic individuals in routine clinical practice are not yet established. Because this patient has a nontwin sibling with a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes, her personal risk of developing the same condition is estimated to be approximately 10%.

The American Diabetes Association recommends that individuals at higher risk, including relatives of patients with type 1 diabetes, be referred for antibody testing and risk assessment in the context of a clinical research study (Answer A). This coordinated screening may enhance understanding of individual risk, provide education regarding diabetes symptoms, and permit close follow-up with perhaps an earlier diabetes diagnosis in those who do progress. Given the potential benefits of trial participation and the absence of firm guidelines for screening in clinical practice, enrollment in a clinical trial would be the preferred option for this young woman. Additional information regarding participation in such trials is available at www.diabetestrialnet.org.

Measurement of hemoglobin  $ A_{1c} $ (Answer B), although a reasonable screening test for type 2 diabetes, is not appropriate method to screen for type 1 diabetes. Promisingly, studies of individuals at high risk do suggest that type 1 diabetes may be predicted on the basis of a variety of parameters. In the Diabetes Prevention Trial of type 1 Diabetes (DPT-1) (a study of islet-cell autoantibody-positive relatives of patients with type 1 diabetes), use a risk score (DPTRS) derived from parameters including BMI, age, fasting C-peptide level, and postchallenge glucose and C-peptide levels appeared to predict type 1 diabetes in that population. However, measurement of its patient's fasting glucose, insulin, and C-peptide levels in the clinical setting (Answer C) is unlikely to be similarly meaningful. In the TrialNet Natural History Study of antibody-positive relatives of individuals with type 1 diabetes, generation of a risk score incorporating levels of multiple islet antibodies has shown promise in predicting diabetes risk. However, there are not yet clear guidelines for the screening of asymptomatic individuals via

autoantibody testing in clinical practice. Thus, in-clinic screening for either the antibody combinations of islet-cell cytoplasmic autoantibodies and glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies (Answer D) or insulinoma-2-associated autoantibodies and zinc transporter 8 autoantibodies (Answer E) would not be the preferred next steps. Continued engagement of high-risk individuals in clinical trials is the best means of refining clinical care in the future.

##### Educational Objective:

Provide guideline-based screening recommendations to patients at risk for type 1 diabetes mellitus.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Overview of medical care in adults with diabetes mellitus

##### Reference(s):

American Diabetes Association. Standards of medical care in diabetes--2014. Diabetes Care. 2014;37(Suppl 1):S14-S80.

Sosenko JM, Krischer JP, Palmer JP, et al; Diabetes Prevention Trial-Type 1 Study Group. A risk score for type 1 diabetes derived from autoantibody-positive participants in the diabetes prevention trial-type 1. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(3):528-533.

Sosenko JM, Skyler JS, Palmer JP, et al; Type 1 Diabetes TrialNet Study Group; Diabetes Prevention Trial-Type 1 Study Gro

A 28-year-old woman with a 12-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus is referred to you for management of her diabetes in the setting of preconception planning. She is using continuous subcutaneous infusion of insulin. She usually experiences hypoglycemia twice a month, has good awareness of hypoglycemic symptoms, and has not required outside assistance to manage these episodes. She has never used glucagon. The patient is currently taking an oral contraceptive and vitamin supplementation. She is not taking any other medication. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable.

On physical examination, she has a soft ejection systolic murmur, but there is no ankle edema. There is no atrophy of subcutaneous fat and no bruises or striae. Weight is 160 lb (72.7 kg), and height is 69 in (175.3 cm) (BMI = 23.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and there are no postural changes in blood pressure. Sensation to vibration, monofilament, and temperature is intact. Fundoscopy reveals the presence of microaneurysms in both eyes.

Results of laboratory tests ordered by her primary care physician (sample drawn at 8 AM, fasting):

 $$ \mathrm{Hemoglobin}=11.2\mathrm{g/dL}(112\mathrm{g/L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{M C V}=79\mathrm{\mu m}^{3}(79\mathrm{f L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{White~blood~cell~count}=5700/\mu\mathrm{L}(5.7\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Platelet\ count}=208\times10^{3}/\mu\mathrm{L}(208\times10^{9}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Glucose}=114\mathrm{mg}/\mathrm{dL}(6.33\mathrm{mmol}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Sodium}=138\mathrm{m E q/L}(138\mathrm{~mmol/L}) $$ 

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=6.8\% $ (0.068)

 $$ \mathrm{P o t a s s i u m=4.5~m E q/L~(4.5~m m o l/L)} $$ 

 $$ Creatinine=0.8\ mg/dL(70.7\ \mu mol/L) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{TSH}=2.2\,\mathrm{mIU/L} $$ 

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 18 ng/mL (44.9 nmol/L)

Albumin-to-creatinine ratio = 5 µg/mg

In addition to optimizing her glycemic control, measuring which of the following is the most important next management step?
options:
Glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies A 58-year-old woman sees her oncologist to address a 6-month history of daily headache. Her medicine history includes treatment of estrogen-receptor positive breast cancer 4 years ago. She had clear surgical margins and negative nodes at the time of surgery. She was treated with both radiation and chemotherapy. Anastrozole, 1 mg daily, was prescribed, and she still takes this medication. There has been no evidence of recurrence and mammography of the cancer. Recurrence, and mammography of the contralateral breast has been normal.
In the past several months, she has begun to experience vague, general headaches that occur at random times. She has no history of headaches, and because they have become more frequent, she has sought advice from her oncologist. She has no evidence of polyuria, polydipsia, or nocturia. She has no history of changes in her ring or shoe size. Physical examination findings are unremarkable. There is no evidence of altered visual fields, hyperpigmentation, skin tags, striae, ecchymosis, galactorrhea.
items.explanation:
## 119 ANSWER: B) Tissue transglutaminase antibodies

119 Celiac disease occurs with increased frequency in persons with type 1 diabetes mellitus and in such instances, malabsorption can result in deficiency of iron, folate, and fat-soluble vitamins such as vitamin D. These deficiencies could have adverse effects on maternal and fetal health (especially with respect to folate deficiency and fetal neural tube defects). The presence of microcytosis in a patient with type 1 diabetes and mild anemia should raise the possibility of celiac disease. Measuring tissue transglutaminase antibodies (Answer B) is recognized as an appropriate screening tool in these circumstances and this is the next step for this patient.

On the basis of her physical examination and current laboratory results, measurement of TPO antibodies (Answer A) as a marker of thyroid autoimmunity is unlikely to guide management. She is euthyroid, and with a normal TSH level, the recommendation would be to follow TSH.

Given the patient's unremarkable history, normal physical examination findings, normal serum creatinine concentration, and no microalbuminuria, detailed measures of renal function, such as iothalamate clearance (Answer C), are not indicated.

Because of the patient's age, absence of fracture history, and established diagnosis of diabetes treated with insulin, measurement of bone mineral density (Answer D) or glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies (Answer E) is unlikely to be helpful in her preconception management. In light of the patient's low 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentration, replacement therapy is indicated.

##### Educational Objective:

Suspect celiac disease in a patient with type 1 diabetes mellitus, microcytosis, and mild anemia.

Pathogenesis, epidemiology, and clinical manifestations of celiac disease in adults

Reference(s):

Cronin CC, Feighery A, Ferriss Jb, Liddy C, Shanahan F, Feighery C. High prevalence of celiac disease among patients with insulin-dependent (type I) diabetes mellitus. Am J Gastroenterol. 1997;92(12):2210-2212.
